The ideal ammeter lies in the bottom connection between the lower-right and lower-left nodes.

28. A biconvex lens is formed by using two thin
planoconvex lenses, as shown in the figure. The
refractive index and radius of curved surfaces
are also mentioned in figure. When an object is
placed on the left side of lens at a distance of 30 cm
from the biconvex lens, the magnification of the
image will be :
15cm 12cm
 =1.5  =1.2
(1) –2 (2) +2
(3) +2.5 (4) –2.5

ambiguities: The two planoconvex lenses appear joined along a central vertical planar interface, but the exact contact/adhesive medium is not explicitly labeled.
The image does not explicitly assign the 15 cm and 12 cm radii to signed surfaces; only their geometric association with the left and right curved surfaces is shown.
The small dark triangular marks near the top and lower sides appear to be annotation arrowheads, not physical parts of the lens.
answer_relevant_observations: An object is stated to be placed 30 cm to the left of the composite lens.
The requested quantity is image magnification.
The figure gives different refractive indices for the two constituent planoconvex lenses and different radii for the two outer curved surfaces.
visual_interpretation.diagram_type: Optical ray-system geometry diagram of a composite biconvex lens; no rays, axes, or graph trends are drawn.
entities: Object described in text, not drawn
relationships: Two thin planoconvex lenses are placed together with their planar faces coincident at the central vertical dotted line.
The left component has refractive index μ = 1.5.
The right component has refractive index μ = 1.2.
The left outer curved surface is labeled with radius 15 cm.
The right outer curved surface is labeled with radius 12 cm.
The composite lens is biconvex, with its principal axis implicitly horizontal through the lens center.
The object lies on the left side, 30 cm from the biconvex lens.
visual_interpretation.summary: A thin composite biconvex lens is formed from two planoconvex lenses joined at a central planar interface. The left half has refractive index 1.5 and outer-surface radius 15 cm; the right half has refractive index 1.2 and outer-surface radius 12 cm. An object is located 30 cm to the left. No ray directions, graph axes, plotted trends, or explicit formulas are shown.

and D2
are connected

ambiguities: Diode symbols indicate their bar/cathode sides face inputs A and B; symbol orientation should be verified from the image convention.
answer_relevant_observations: The junction shared by both diodes, the resistor, and output C is the common output node.
A resistor pulls the common output node upward toward a 5 V DC supply.
No input voltage levels, diode drops, or resistor value are specified.
Four printed answer choices are visible, but no answer is selected.
visual_interpretation.diagram_type: Electronic circuit schematic for a two-input diode logic network
relationships: The 5 V DC supply terminal connects vertically downward through resistor R to the common junction.
Input A connects horizontally through diode D1 to the common junction.
Input B connects horizontally through diode D2 to the common junction.
The right side of D1 joins the vertical conductor at the common junction.
The right side of D2 lies on the lower horizontal conductor, which joins the same vertical conductor.
Output C extends horizontally rightward from the common junction and ends at an open-circle terminal.
D1 and D2 are drawn horizontally and parallel, with their bar/cathode sides toward the left-side inputs A and B and their opposite sides toward the common output node.
The resistor and supply form an upward pull-up branch from the common output node.
visual_interpretation.summary: A two-input diode-resistor logic circuit is shown. Inputs A and B each connect through a left-facing diode, D1 and D2 respectively, to one common node. That node is connected to output C and pulled up through resistor R to a 5 V DC supply.

–1.24 mm, 1.25 mm, 1.23 mm, 1.21 mm. The

recording these readings is _____mm.
(1) 0.01 (2) 0.001
(3) 0.1 (4) 0.05

coordinate is given as x(t) = 4t3
– 3t.
A. The particle returns to its original position
(origin) 0.866 units later
B. The particle is 1 unit away from origin at its
turning point.

turning point.

non-negative.

below :
(1) A,C,D only (2) A,B,C only
(3) C,E only (4) A,C only

is 400 m/s. If the density and Young's modulus of
the bar material are increased by 0.5% and 1%
respectively then the speed of the wave is changed
approximately to _____ m/s.
(1) 399 (2) 398 (3) 402 (4) 401

of capacitors is always smaller than the smallest
capacitance of the capacitor in the combination.

the charged plates of a capacitor, displacement
of charges cannot occur due to insulation
property of dielectric.

of thickness of dielectric is an alternate method to
increase the capacitance.

centered at the location of the charge are
equipotential surfaces.

below.
(1) A, B and C only
(2) C and D only
(3) A, C and D only
(4) B and D only

48. An inductor stores 16 J of magnetic field energy
and dissipates 32 W of thermal energy due to its
resistance when an a.c. current of 2 A (rms) and
frequency 50 Hz flows through it. The ratio of
inductive reactance to its resistance is ________.
( = 3.14)

49. A beam of light consisting of wavelengths 650 nm
and 550 nm illuminates the Young’s double slits
with separation of 2 mm such that the interference

of 1.2 m from the slits. The least distance of a point
from the central maximum, where the bright
fringes due to both the wavelengths coincide, is
______ × 10–5
m.

having negligible mass is wound around the wheel
and the loose end of the string is connected to a
3 kg mass. The mass is kept at rest initially and
released. Kinetic energy of the wheel when the
mass descends by 3 m is ______ J. (g = 10 m/s2)

community_summaries.summary_text: Theme: Physics. Concepts: Alternating Current, Atoms and Nuclei, Current Electricity, Dual Nature of Matter, Electromagnetic Induction. Prerequisites: Kinetic Theory, Laws of Motion, Current Electricity, Atoms and Nuclei, Ray Optics. Misconceptions: Check the units, sign convention, and limiting case before locking in the option., Check the units, sign convention, and limiting case before locking in the option., Use kelvin for temperatures and make sure you are balancing heat, work, or state variables from the same process., Use the balance condition as a ratio of resistances to wire lengths, and re-check which arm changed after adding the ext, Reduce the circuit stepwise and verify whether each part is in series or parallel after every change..

Theme: Units and Measurements. Concepts: SI units, SI units problem application, Units and Measurements, absolute error, absolute error problem application. Prerequisites: Units and Measurements, solve units and measurements problems, apply electromagnetic induction. Misconceptions: Check the units, sign convention, and limiting case before locking in the option., When fields from multiple charges are involved, add vectors component-wise and watch the sign of each charge., Track every phase change and use the correct specific heat for each phase before writing the heat balance., At terminal speed the net force is zero, so magnetic drag must balance weight before you solve for v., Check the units, sign convention, and limiting case before locking in the option..

Misconceptions: At terminal speed the net force is zero, so magnetic drag must balance weight before you solve for v., Reduce the circuit stepwise and verify whether each part is in series or parallel after every change., When fields from multiple charges are involved, add vectors component-wise and watch the sign of each charge., At terminal speed the net force is zero, so magnetic drag must balance weight before you solve for v., Check the equivalent combination carefully; series and parallel capacitance rules are easy to swap..

Chapter Two ELECTROSTATIC POTENTIAL AND CAPACITANCE 2.1 INTRODUCTION In Chapters 5 and 7 (Class XI), the notion of potential energy was introduced. When an external force does work in taking a body from a point to another against a force like spring force or gravitational force, that work gets stored as potential energy of the body. When the external force is removed, the body moves, gaining kinetic energy and losing an equal amount of potential energy. The sum of kinetic and potential energies is thus conserved. Forces of this kind are called conservative forces. Spring force and gravitational force are examples of conservative forces. Coulomb force between two (stationary) charges is also

Chapter Three CURRENT ELECTRICITY 3.1 INTRODUCTION In Chapter 1, all charges whether free or bound, were considered to be at rest. Charges in motion constitute an electric current. Such currents occur naturally in many situations. Lightning is one such phenomenon in which charges flow from the clouds to the earth through the atmosphere, sometimes with disastrous results. The flow of charges in lightning is not steady, but in our everyday life we see many devices where charges flow in a steady manner, like water flowing smoothly in a river. A torch and a cell-driven clock are examples of such devices. In the present chapter, we shall study some of the basic laws concerning steady electric cur

Chapter Four MOVING CHARGES AND MAGNETISM 4.1 INTRODUCTION Both Electricity and Magnetism have been known for more than 2000 years. However, it was only about 200 years ago, in 1820, that it was realised that they were intimately related. During a lecture demonstration in the summer of 1820, Danish physicist Hans Christian Oersted noticed that a current in a straight wire caused a noticeable deflection in a nearby magnetic compass needle. He investigated this phenomenon. He found that the alignment of the needle is tangential to an imaginary circle which has the straight wire as its centre and has its plane perpendicular to the wire. Physics Chapter Five MAGNETISM AND MATTER 5.1 INTRODUCTION Magnetic phenomena are universal in nature. Vast, distant galaxies, the tiny invisible atoms, humans and beasts all are permeated through and through with a host of magnetic fields from a variety of sources. The earth’s magnetism predates human evolution. The word magnet is derived from the name of an island in Greece called magnesia where magnetic ore deposits were found, as early as 600 BC. In the previous chapter we have learned that moving charges or electric currents produce magnetic fields. This discovery, which was made in the early part of the nineteenth century is credited to Oersted, Ampere, Biot and Savart, among others. I

Physics Chapter Six ELECTROMAGNETIC INDUCTION 6.1 INTRODUCTION Electricity and magnetism were considered separate and unrelated phenomena for a long time. In the early decades of the nineteenth century, experiments on electric current by Oersted, Ampere and a few others established the fact that electricity and magnetism are inter-related. They found that moving electric charges produce magnetic fields. For example, an electric current deflects a magnetic compass needle placed in its vicinity. This naturally raises the questions like: Is the converse effect possible? Can moving magnets produce electric currents? Does the nature permit such a relation between electricity and magnetism? Chapter Seven ALTERNATING CURRENT 7.1 INTRODUCTION We have so far considered direct current (dc) sources and circuits with dc sources. These currents do not change direction with time. But voltages and currents that vary with time are very common. The electric mains supply in our homes and offices is a voltage that varies like a sine function with time. Such a voltage is called alternating voltage (ac voltage) and the current driven by it in a circuit is called the alternating current (ac current)*. Today, most of the electrical devices we use require ac voltage. This is mainly because most of the electrical energy sold by power companies is transmitted and distributed as alternating current

Chapter Eight ELECTROMAGNETIC WAVES 8.1 INTRODUCTION In Chapter 4, we learnt that an electric current produces magnetic field and that two current-carrying wires exert a magnetic force on each other. Further, in Chapter 6, we have seen that a magnetic field changing with time gives rise to an electric field. Is the converse also true? Does an electric field changing with time give rise to a magnetic field? James Clerk Maxwell (1831-1879), argued that this was indeed the case – not only an electric current but also a time-varying electric field generates magnetic field. Chapter Nine RAY OPTICS AND OPTICAL INSTRUMENTS 9.1 INTRODUCTION Nature has endowed the human eye (retina) with the sensitivity to detect electromagnetic waves within a small range of the electromagnetic spectrum. Electromagnetic radiation belonging to this region of the spectrum (wavelength of about 400 nm to 750 nm) is called light. It is mainly through light and the sense of vision that we know and interpret the world around us. There are two things that we can intuitively mention about light from common experience. Wave Optics Chapter Ten WAVE OPTICS 10.1 INTRODUCTION In 1637 Descartes gave the corpuscular model of light and derived Snell’s law. It explained the laws of reflection and refraction of light at an interface. The corpuscular model predicted that if the ray of light (on refraction) bends towards the normal then the speed of light would be greater in the second medium. This corpuscular model of light was further developed by Isaac Newton in his famous book entitled OPTICKS and because of the tremendous popularity of this book, the corpuscular model is very often attributed to Newton. Towards the same period at the end of 19th century, experimental investigations on conduction of electricity (electric discharge) through gases at low pressure in a discharge tube led to many historic discoveries. The discovery of X-rays by Roentgen in 1895, and of electron by J. J. Thomson in 1897, were important milestones in the understanding of atomic structure. official_answer: (4)
question_text:
33. given below are two statements:
statement i : pressure of fluid is exerted only on a
solid surface in contact as the fluid-pressure does
not exist everywhere in a still fluid.
statement ii: excess potential energy of the
molecules on the surface of a liquid, when
compared to interior, results in surface tension.
in the light of the above statements, choose the
correct answer from the options given below.
(1) statement i is true but statement ii is false
(2) both statement i and statement ii are false
(3) both statement i and statement ii are true
(4) statement i is false but statement ii is true
solution_text:
according to pascal's law pressure at any point in
liquid at rest is same in all direction.
it exist at every point in the liquid not just at
boundaries. so statement (1) is false.
for interior molecule net cohesive forces are zero

assertion (a) and the other is labelled as reason (r).
consider a ferromagnetic material :
assertion (a) : the individual atoms in a
ferromagnetic material possess a magnetic dipole
moment and interact with one another in such a
way that they spontaneously align themselves
forming domains.
reason (r) : at high enough temperature, the
domain structure of ferromagnetic material
disintegrates. thus, magnetization will disappear at
high enough temperature known as curie
temperature.
in the light of the above statements, choose the
correct answer from the options given below :
(1) (a) is true but (r) is false

question_text:
45. given below are two statements : one is labelled as
assertion (a) and the other is labelled as reason (r).
consider a ferromagnetic material :
assertion (a) : the individual atoms in a
ferromagnetic material possess a magnetic dipole
moment and interact with one another in such a
way that they spontaneously align themselves
forming domains.
reason (r) : at high enough temperature, the
domain structure of ferromagnetic material
disintegrates. thus, magnetization will disappear at
high enough temperature known as curie
temperature.
in the light of the above statements, choose the
correct answer from the options given below :
(1) (a) is true but (r) is false
(2) both (a) and (r) are true but (r) is not the
correct explanation of (a)
(3) both (a) and (r) are true and (r) is the correct
explanation of (a)
(4) (a) is false but (r) is true

question_text:
33. Given below are two statements:
Statement I : Pressure of fluid is exerted only on a
solid surface in contact as the fluid-pressure does
not exist everywhere in a still fluid.
Statement II: Excess potential energy of the
molecules on the surface of a liquid, when
compared to interior, results in surface tension.
In the light of the above statements, choose the
correct answer from the options given below.
(1) Statement I is true but Statement II is false
(2) Both Statement I and Statement II are false
(3) Both Statement I and Statement II are true
(4) Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution_text:
According to pascal's law pressure at any point in
liquid at rest is same in all direction.
It exist at every point in the liquid not just at
boundaries. So statement (1) is false.
For interior molecule net cohesive forces are zero
statement (2) is correct.
pitfalls:
- Pressure is scalar and acts equally in all directions; do not treat it as a vector."}

33. A current carrying is placed vertically and a
particle of mass m with charge Q is released from
rest. The particle moves along the axis of solenoid.
If g is acceleration due to gravity then the
acceleration (a) of the charged particle will satisfy :
(1) a = g (2) a > g
(3) a = 0 (4) 0 < a < g
solution_text:
Since the solenoid is placed vertically, the
magnetic field inside the solenoid will be either
along – y or +y axis.

38. Given below are two statements:
Statement I : For all elements, greater the mass of
the nucleus, greater is the binding energy per
nucleon.
Statement II : For all elements, nuclei with less
binding energy per nucleon transforms to nuclei
with greater binding energy per nucleon.
In the light of the above statements, choose the
correct answer from the options given below:
(1) Both Statement I and Statement II are true
(2) Statement I is true but Statement II is false
(3) Both Statement I and Statement II are false
(4) Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution_text:

pitfalls:

- At closest approach, kinetic energy converts into electrostatic potential energy; use that energy balance directly."}

normalized_documents.normalized_text: # detailed step-by-step solutions: iit jee mains physics **past year questions analysis** this document contains detailed step-by-step solutions for selected physics questions from the provided iit jee mains past papers (january 21 & 22, 2026). each solution is accompanied by the core reasoning, potential pitfalls (to help avoid common mistakes), and the final answer. --- ## paper reference: jee-main session-1 (22-01-2026 / morning shift) ### question id: jee-2026-01-22-q26 **topic:** rotational mechanics (moment of inertia) **question:** a solid sphere of mass 5 kg and radius 10 cm is kept in contact with another solid sphere of mass 10 kg and radius 20 cm. the moment of inertia of this pair of spheres about the tangent passing through the point of contact is (in $kg \cdot m^2$): (1) 0.36 (2) 0.72 (3) 0.18 (4) 0.63 **step-by-step solution:** 1. **understand the geometry:** we have two solid spheres touching each other. the axis of rotation is the common tangent at their point of contact. 2. **identify the formula:** for a single solid sphere of mass $m$ and radius $r$, the moment of inertia about its center of mass is $i_{cm} = rac{2}{5}mr^2$. 3. **apply parallel axis theorem:** the common tangent is parallel to the central axes of both spheres and is at a distance $r_1$ from the center of sphere 1 and $r_2$ from the center of sphere 2. * $i_1 = i_{cm1} + m_1 r_1^2 = rac{2}{5}m_1 r_1^2 + m_1 r_1^2 = rac{7}{5}m_1 r_1^2$ * $i_2 = i_{cm2} + m_2 r_2^2 = rac{2}{5}m_2 r_2^2 + m_2 r_2^2 = rac{7}{5}m_2 r_2^2$ 4. **calculate total moi:** $i_{total} = i_1 + i_2 = rac{7}{5} (m_1 r_1^2 + m_2 r_2^2)$ 5. **substitute values:** * $m_1 = 5$ kg, $r_1 = 10$ cm = $0.1$ m * $m_2 = 10$ kg, $r_2 = 20$ cm = $0.2$ m $i_{total} = 1.4 imes [5(0.1)^2 + 10(0.2)^2]$ $i_{total} = 1.4 imes [5(0.01) + 10(0.04)]$ $i_{total} = 1.4 imes [0.05 + 0.40] = 1.4 imes 0.45 = 0.63 \, kg \cdot m^2$ **common pitfalls & misconceptions:** * **wrong base formula:** using the formula for a hollow sphere ($ rac{2}{3}mr^2$) or a disc ($ rac{1}{2}mr^2$) instead of a solid sphere ($ rac{2}{5}mr^2$). * **forgetting the parallel axis theorem:** students often just calculate the moi about the centers of mass and add them up, completely ignoring the shift of the axis to the tangent. * **unit conversion errors:** forgetting to convert the radii from cm to meters before squaring. **final answer:** (4) 0.63 --- ### question id: jee-2026-01-22-q29 **topic:** electromagnetic induction (motional emf) **question:** xpqy is a vertical smooth long loop having a total resistance r where px is parallel to qy and separation between them is $l$. a constant magnetic field b perpendicular to the plane of the loop exists in the entire space. a rod cd of length l ($l > l$) and mass m is made to slide down from rest under gravity. the terminal speed acquired by the rod is: (1) $ rac{2mgr}{b^2l^2}$ (2) $ rac{8mgr}{b^2l^2}$ (3) $ rac{2mgr}{b^2l^2}$ (4) $ rac{mgr}{b^2l^2}$ **step-by-step solution:** 1. **analyze the forces:** as the rod falls under gravity, its velocity $v$ increases. this downward motion through the magnetic field $b$ generates a motional emf. 2. **calculate induced emf and current:** * motional emf across the rails: $ arepsilon = bvl$ (note: only the length $l$ between the rails contributes to the circuit). * induced current: $i = rac{ arepsilon}{r} = rac{bvl}{r}$. 3. **determine magnetic force:** the current-carrying rod experiences an upward magnetic force (lenz's law opposes the change, which is the falling motion). * $f_m = i( ec{l} imes ec{b}) = i l b = \left( rac{bvl}{r} ight) l b = rac{b^2 l^2 v}{r}$. 4. **condition for terminal speed:** terminal speed is reached when the net force on the rod is zero. * $f_{net} = mg - f_m = 0 \implies mg = rac{b^2 l^2 v}{r}$ 5. **solve for $v$:** * $v = rac{mgr}{b^2l^2}$ **common pitfalls & misconceptions:** * **using the wrong length:** the rod has total length $l$, but the separation between the rails is $l$. the emf and the subsequent magnetic force only occur over the length $l$ that is actively part of the closed circuit. using $l$ instead of $l$ leads to option (3), which is a classic trap. * **sign direction mistake:** misapplying fleming's rules and thinking the magnetic force pushes downwards, which would make terminal velocity impossible. **final answer:** (4) $ rac{mgr}{b^2l^2}$ --- ### question id: jee-2026-01-22-q30 **topic:** gravitation (escape velocity) **question:** the escape velocity from a spherical planet a is $10 \, km/s$. the escape velocity from another planet b whose density and radius are 10% of those of planet a, is: (1) 1000 m/s (2) $200\sqrt{5}$ m/s (3) $100\sqrt{10}$ m/s (4) $1000\sqrt{2}$ m/s **step-by-step solution:** 1. **recall escape velocity formula:** standard formula is $v_e = \sqrt{ rac{2gm}{r}}$. 2. **express in terms of density ($ ho$):** since the problem gives ratios of density and radius, substitute $m = ext{volume} imes ext{density} = rac{4}{3}\pi r^3 ho$. * $v_e = \sqrt{ rac{2g}{r} \left( rac{4}{3}\pi r^3 ho ight)} = \sqrt{ rac{8}{3}\pi g ho r^2} = r \sqrt{ rac{8}{3}\pi g ho}$ * therefore, $v_e \propto r \sqrt{ ho}$ 3. **set up ratio:** * $ rac{v_{eb}}{v_{ea}} = rac{r_b \sqrt{ ho_b}}{r_a \sqrt{ ho_a}} = \left( rac{r_b}{r_a} ight) \sqrt{ rac{ ho_b}{ ho_a}}$ 4. **substitute given values:** * $r_b = 0.1 r_a$ and $ ho_b = 0.1 ho_a$ * $ rac{v_{eb}}{10 \, km/s} = (0.1) \sqrt{0.1} = rac{1}{10} imes rac{1}{\sqrt{10}}$ 5. **calculate final velocity:** * $v_{eb} = 10 imes rac{1}{10\sqrt{10}} \, km/s = rac{1}{\sqrt{10}} \, km/s$ * convert to m/s: $v_{eb} = rac{1000}{\sqrt{10}} = 100\sqrt{10} \, m/s$. **common pitfalls & misconceptions:** * **wrong proportionality:** assuming $v_e \propto rac{m}{r}$ directly without breaking $m$ down into density and volume. if a student assumes $v_e \propto \sqrt{ ho r}$ instead of $r\sqrt{ ho}$, they will get the wrong scaling factor. * **unit errors:** forgetting to convert the final answer from km/s to m/s, matching the options. **final answer:** (3) $100\sqrt{10}$ m/s --- ## paper reference: jee-main session-1 (21-01-2026 / morning shift) ### question id: jee-2026-01-21-q27 **topic:** thermodynamics & calorimetry **question:** a gas based geyser heats water flowing at the rate of 5.0 litres per minute from $27^\circ c$ to $87^\circ c$. the rate of consumption of the gas is (in g/s): (take heat of combustion of gas = $5.0 imes 10^4$ j/g, specific heat capacity of water = $4200$ j/kg·°c) (1) 2.1 (2) 4.2 (3) 0.42 (4) 0.21 **step-by-step solution:** 1. **calculate water mass flow rate:** * volume flow rate = $5.0 \, l/min$. * since the density of water is $pprox 1 \, kg/l$, mass flow rate $ rac{dm}{dt} = 5.0 \, kg/min$. * convert to standard si units (kg/s): $ rac{dm}{dt} = rac{5.0}{60} \, kg/s = rac{1}{12} \, kg/s$. 2. **calculate required heating power:** * temperature change $\delta t = 87^\circ c - 27^\circ c = 60^\circ c$. * required power $p_{req} = rac{dm}{dt} \cdot c \cdot \delta t = \left( rac{1}{12} ight) imes 4200 imes 60$ * $p_{req} = 4200 imes 5 = 21000 \, j/s$ (or 21 kw). 3. **relate power to gas consumption:** * let the rate of gas consumption be $x \, g/s$. * heat provided by gas per second $p_{gas} = x imes ( ext{heat of combustion}) = x imes 5.0 imes 10^4 \, j/s$. 4. **equate and solve:** * $x imes 50000 = 21000$ * $x = rac{21000}{50000} = rac{21}{50} = 0.42 \, g/s$. **common pitfalls & misconceptions:** * **unit inconsistencies:** the most common mistake here is failing to convert minutes to seconds for the water flow rate, leading to an answer that is exactly 60 times too large (or too small depending on where the factor is applied). * **density of water:** forgetting that 1 litre of water = 1 kg. **final answer:** (3) 0.42 --- ### question id: jee-2026-01-21-q28 **topic:** alternating current & emi **question:** a conducting circular loop of area $1.0 \, m^2$ is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field which varies as $b = \sin(100 t)$ tesla. if the resistance of the loop is $100 \, \omega$, then the average thermal energy dissipated in the loop in one period is (in j): (1) $ rac{\pi}{2}$ (2) $2\pi$ (3) $\pi$ (4) $\pi^2$ **step-by-step solution:** 1. **calculate magnetic flux:** * $\phi = b \cdot a = \sin(100t) imes 1.0 = \sin(100t) \, wb$. 2. **calculate induced emf (faraday's law):** * $ arepsilon = - rac{d\phi}{dt} = - rac{d}{dt}[\sin(100t)] = -100 \cos(100t) \, v$. 3. **calculate instantaneous power:** * $p(t) = rac{ arepsilon^2}{r} = rac{[-100 \cos(100t)]^2}{100} = rac{10000 \cos^2(100t)}{100} = 100 \cos^2(100t) \, w$. 4. **determine the time period:** * the angular frequency is $\omega = 100 \, rad/s$. * time period $t = rac{2\pi}{\omega} = rac{2\pi}{100} = rac{\pi}{50} \, s$. 5. **calculate total energy dissipated in one period:** * $e = \int_0^t p(t) dt = \int_0^{\pi/50} 100 \cos^2(100t) dt$. * we know the average value of $\cos^2( heta)$ over a full cycle is $ rac{1}{2}$. * therefore, average power $\langle p angle = 100 imes rac{1}{2} = 50 \, w$. * total energy $e = \langle p angle imes t = 50 imes rac{\pi}{50} = \pi \, joules$. **common pitfalls & misconceptions:** * **differentiation chain rule:** forgetting to multiply by the inner derivative (100) when differentiating $\sin(100t)$, resulting in an emf that is 100 times too small. * **integrating over 1 second instead of 1 period:** the prompt specifies "in one period". some students automatically calculate average power and assume energy = power (which is true for 1 second, but not 1 period). * **trigonometric average:** not knowing that $\int_0^t \cos^2(\omega t) dt = rac{t}{2}$, which forces students to manually expand $\cos^2$ into $ rac{1+\cos(2 heta)}{2}$ and waste valuable exam time. **final answer:** (3) $\pi$ --- ### question id: jee-2026-01-21-q29 **topic:** fluid mechanics (bernoulli's principle & continuity equation) **question:** water flows through a horizontal tube. the difference in height between the water columns in vertical tubes is 5 cm and the area of cross-sections at a and b are $6 \, cm^2$ and $3 \, cm^2$ respectively. the rate of flow will be (in $cm^3/s$, take $g=10 \, m/s^2$): (1) $ rac{200}{\sqrt{3}}$ (2) $200\sqrt{6}$ (3) $200\sqrt{3}$ (4) $100\sqrt{3}$ **step-by-step solution:** 1. **apply continuity equation:** * $a_a v_a = a_b v_b$ * $6 \cdot v_a = 3 \cdot v_b \implies v_b = 2v_a$ 2. **apply bernoulli's equation:** * for a horizontal tube, potential energy head is constant. * $p_a + rac{1}{2} ho v_a^2 = p_b + rac{1}{2} ho v_b^2$ * $p_a - p_b = rac{1}{2} ho (v_b^2 - v_a^2)$ 3. **relate pressure difference to height difference:** * the pressure difference manifests as the height difference $h$ in the vertical tubes: $p_a - p_b = ho g h$. * $ ho g h = rac{1}{2} ho ((2v_a)^2 - v_a^2) = rac{1}{2} ho (3v_a^2)$ * $gh = rac{3}{2} v_a^2 \implies v_a = \sqrt{ rac{2gh}{3}}$ 4. **mind the units!** * $g = 10 \, m/s^2 = 1000 \, cm/s^2$ * $h = 5 \, cm$ * $v_a = \sqrt{ rac{2 imes 1000 imes 5}{3}} = \sqrt{ rac{10000}{3}} = rac{100}{\sqrt{3}} \, cm/s$ 5. **calculate flow rate (q):** * $q = a_a v_a = 6 imes rac{100}{\sqrt{3}} = rac{600}{\sqrt{3}} = 200\sqrt{3} \, cm^3/s$ **common pitfalls & misconceptions:** * **unit mismatch with gravity:** often students plug in $g=10$ while keeping heights and areas in cm. this mixes meters and centimeters, yielding wild numbers like $\sqrt{100/3}$. $g$ must be converted to $cm/s^2$ (i.e., 1000) if working entirely in cgs. * **calculation slip:** squaring $2v_a$ as $2v_a^2$ instead of $4v_a^2$, which leads to $v_b^2 - v_a^2 = v_a^2$ instead of $3v_a^2$. **final answer:** (3) $200\sqrt{3}$ --- ### question id: jee-2026-01-21-q31 **topic:** kinematics & newton's laws (variable force) **question:** a 4 kg mass moves under the influence of a force $ ec{f} = (4t^3 \hat{i} - 3t \hat{j}) \, n$ where $t$ is the time in second. if mass starts from origin at $t=0$, the velocity and position after $t=2s$ will be: (1) $ ec{v} = 3\hat{i} + rac{3}{2}\hat{j}$, $ ec{r} = rac{6}{5}\hat{i} + \hat{j}$ (2) $ ec{v} = 4\hat{i} - rac{3}{2}\hat{j}$, $ ec{r} = rac{8}{5}\hat{i} - \hat{j}$ (3) $ ec{v} = 4\hat{i} + rac{5}{2}\hat{j}$, $ ec{r} = rac{8}{5}\hat{i} + 2\hat{j}$ (4) $ ec{v} = 4\hat{i} - rac{3}{2}\hat{j}$, $ ec{r} = rac{6}{5}\hat{i} - \hat{j}$ **step-by-step solution:** 1. **find acceleration vector:** * $ ec{a} = rac{ ec{f}}{m} = rac{4t^3 \hat{i} - 3t \hat{j}}{4} = t^3 \hat{i} - rac{3}{4}t \hat{j} \, m/s^2$. 2. **integrate to find velocity:** * $ ec{v}(t) = \int ec{a} \, dt = \int (t^3 \hat{i} - rac{3}{4}t \hat{j}) \, dt = rac{t^4}{4}\hat{i} - rac{3}{8}t^2 \hat{j} + c$. * since it starts from origin from rest (implied by "starts from origin" combined with standard conventions if initial v isn't stated, though strictly "starts" implies $v(0)=0$), $c = 0$. * at $t = 2s$: $ ec{v}(2) = rac{2^4}{4}\hat{i} - rac{3}{8}(2^2)\hat{j} = rac{16}{4}\hat{i} - rac{12}{8}\hat{j} = 4\hat{i} - 1.5\hat{j} = 4\hat{i} - rac{3}{2}\hat{j} \, m/s$. 3. **integrate to find position:** * $ ec{r}(t) = \int ec{v} \, dt = \int ( rac{t^4}{4}\hat{i} - rac{3}{8}t^2 \hat{j}) \, dt = rac{t^5}{20}\hat{i} - rac{3}{24}t^3 \hat{j} + c'$. * starts at origin, so $ ec{r}(0) = 0 \implies c' = 0$. * at $t = 2s$: $ ec{r}(2) = rac{2^5}{20}\hat{i} - rac{1}{8}(2^3)\hat{j} = rac{32}{20}\hat{i} - rac{8}{8}\hat{j} = rac{8}{5}\hat{i} - \hat{j} \, m$. 4. **match with options:** * $ ec{v} = 4\hat{i} - rac{3}{2}\hat{j}$ and $ ec{r} = rac{8}{5}\hat{i} - \hat{j}$ matches option (2). **common pitfalls & misconceptions:** * **integration errors:** basic polynomial integration errors, especially forgetting to divide by the new exponent ($n+1$). * **forgetting to divide force by mass:** many students integrate the force vector directly, assuming it is the acceleration vector. always verify $ ec{a} = ec{f}/m$. **final answer:** (2) $ ec{v} = 4\hat{i} - rac{3}{2}\hat{j}$, $ ec{r} = rac{8}{5}\hat{i} - \hat{j}$ --- ### question id: jee-2026-01-21-q32 **topic:** units and dimensions **question:** consider a modified bernoulli equation: $(p + rac{a}{bt^2}) + ho g (h + bt) + rac{1}{2} ho v^2 = ext{constant}$ if $t$ has the dimension of time, then the dimensions of a and b are: (1) $[ml^0t^{-1}]$ and $[m^0lt]$ (2) $[ml^0t^{-1}]$ and $[m^0lt^{-1}]$ (3) $[ml^0t^{-2}]$ and $[m^0lt^{-2}]$ (4) $[ml^0t^{-2}]$ and $[m^0lt^{-1}]$ **step-by-step solution:** 1. **principle of dimensional homogeneity:** only physical quantities with the same dimensions can be added or subtracted. 2. **determine dimension of b:** * look at the term $(h + bt)$. $h$ is height, so it has dimensions of length $[l]$. * therefore, $bt$ must also have dimensions of length $[l]$. * $[b] \cdot [t] = [l] \implies [b] = [l t^{-1}]$. (this eliminates options 1 and 3). 3. **determine dimension of a:** * look at the term $(p + rac{a}{bt^2})$. $p$ is pressure, which has dimensions $ rac{ ext{force}}{ ext{area}} = rac{[mlt^{-2}]}{[l^2]} = [ml^{-1}t^{-2}]$. * therefore, $ rac{a}{bt^2}$ must also have dimensions of pressure $[ml^{-1}t^{-2}]$. * $ rac{[a]}{[l t^{-1}] \cdot [t^2]} = [ml^{-1}t^{-2}]$ * $ rac{[a]}{[lt]} = [ml^{-1}t^{-2}]$ * $[a] = [ml^{-1}t^{-2}] \cdot [lt] = [m l^{-1+1} t^{-2+1}] = [m l^0 t^{-1}]$. 4. **match with options:** * $[a] = [ml^0t^{-1}]$ and $[b] = [m^0lt^{-1}]$ matches option (2). **common pitfalls & misconceptions:** * **overcomplicating the algebra:** expanding the whole bernoulli expression instead of simply isolating the terms being added inside the brackets. the bracket $(h+bt)$ is the fastest way to find $b$. * **recalling dimensions incorrectly:** forgetting the dimensions of pressure ($p = f/a$) is a frequent failure point. **final answer:** (2) $[ml^0t^{-1}]$ and $[m^0lt^{-1}]$

**Past Year Questions Analysis**

This document contains detailed step-by-step solutions for selected physics questions from the provided IIT JEE Mains past papers (January 21 & 22, 2026). Each solution is accompanied by the core reasoning, potential pitfalls (to help avoid common mistakes), and the final answer.

path: Detailed Step-by-Step Solutions: IIT JEE Mains Physics
Paper Reference: JEE-Main Session-1 (22-01-2026 / Morning Shift)
sections.text:
**Topic:** Rotational Mechanics (Moment of Inertia)
**Question:** A solid sphere of mass 5 kg and radius 10 cm is kept in contact with another solid sphere of mass 10 kg and radius 20 cm. The moment of inertia of this pair of spheres about the tangent passing through the point of contact is (in $kg \cdot m^2$):
(1) 0.36
(2) 0.72
(3) 0.18
(4) 0.63

**Step-by-Step Solution:**
1.  **Understand the Geometry:** We have two solid spheres touching each other. The axis of rotation is the common tangent at their point of contact.
2.  **Identify the Formula:** For a single solid sphere of mass $m$ and radius $R$, the moment of inertia about its center of mass is $I_{cm} =  rac{2}{5}mR^2$.
3.  **Apply Parallel Axis Theorem:** The common tangent is parallel to the central axes of both spheres and is at a distance $R_1$ from the center of sphere 1 and $R_2$ from the center of sphere 2.
* $I_1 = I_{cm1} + m_1 R_1^2 =  rac{2}{5}m_1 R_1^2 + m_1 R_1^2 =  rac{7}{5}m_1 R_1^2$
* $I_2 = I_{cm2} + m_2 R_2^2 =  rac{2}{5}m_2 R_2^2 + m_2 R_2^2 =  rac{7}{5}m_2 R_2^2$
4.  **Calculate Total MOI:** $I_{total} = I_1 + I_2 =  rac{7}{5} (m_1 R_1^2 + m_2 R_2^2)$
5.  **Substitute Values:**
* $m_1 = 5$ kg, $R_1 = 10$ cm = $0.1$ m
* $m_2 = 10$ kg, $R_2 = 20$ cm = $0.2$ m
$I_{total} = 1.4 	imes [5(0.1)^2 + 10(0.2)^2]$
$I_{total} = 1.4 	imes [5(0.01) + 10(0.04)]$
$I_{total} = 1.4 	imes [0.05 + 0.40] = 1.4 	imes 0.45 = 0.63 \, kg \cdot m^2$

**Common Pitfalls & Misconceptions:**
* **Wrong Base Formula:** Using the formula for a hollow sphere ($ rac{2}{3}mR^2$) or a disc ($ rac{1}{2}mR^2$) instead of a solid sphere ($ rac{2}{5}mR^2$).
* **Forgetting the Parallel Axis Theorem:** Students often just calculate the MOI about the centers of mass and add them up, completely ignoring the shift of the axis to the tangent.
* **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert the radii from cm to meters before squaring.

**Final Answer:** (4) 0.63

---
sections.title: Question ID: JEE-2026-01-22-Q26

path: Detailed Step-by-Step Solutions: IIT JEE Mains Physics
Paper Reference: JEE-Main Session-1 (22-01-2026 / Morning Shift)
sections.text:
**Topic:** Electromagnetic Induction (Motional EMF)
**Question:** XPQY is a vertical smooth long loop having a total resistance R where PX is parallel to QY and separation between them is $l$. A constant magnetic field B perpendicular to the plane of the loop exists in the entire space. A rod CD of length L ($L > l$) and mass m is made to slide down from rest under gravity. The terminal speed acquired by the rod is:
(1) $ rac{2mgR}{B^2l^2}$
(2) $ rac{8mgR}{B^2l^2}$
(3) $ rac{2mgR}{B^2L^2}$
(4) $ rac{mgR}{B^2l^2}$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**
1.  **Analyze the Forces:** As the rod falls under gravity, its velocity $v$ increases. This downward motion through the magnetic field $B$ generates a motional EMF.
2.  **Calculate Induced EMF and Current:** * Motional EMF across the rails: $arepsilon = Bvl$ (Note: only the length $l$ between the rails contributes to the circuit).
* Induced Current: $I =  rac{arepsilon}{R} =  rac{Bvl}{R}$.
3.  **Determine Magnetic Force:** The current-carrying rod experiences an upward magnetic force (Lenz's Law opposes the change, which is the falling motion).
* $F_m = I(ec{l} 	imes ec{B}) = I l B = \left( rac{Bvl}{R}
ight) l B =  rac{B^2 l^2 v}{R}$.
4.  **Condition for Terminal Speed:** Terminal speed is reached when the net force on the rod is zero.
* $F_{net} = mg - F_m = 0 \implies mg =  rac{B^2 l^2 v}{R}$
5.  **Solve for $v$:**
* $v =  rac{mgR}{B^2l^2}$

**Common Pitfalls & Misconceptions:**
* **Using the wrong length:** The rod has total length $L$, but the separation between the rails is $l$. The EMF and the subsequent magnetic force only occur over the length $l$ that is actively part of the closed circuit. Using $L$ instead of $l$ leads to option (3), which is a classic trap.
* **Sign direction mistake:** Misapplying Fleming's rules and thinking the magnetic force pushes downwards, which would make terminal velocity impossible.

**Final Answer:** (4) $ rac{mgR}{B^2l^2}$

---

**Topic:** Gravitation (Escape Velocity)
**Question:** The escape velocity from a spherical planet A is $10 \, km/s$. The escape velocity from another planet B whose density and radius are 10% of those of planet A, is:
(1) 1000 m/s
(2) $200\sqrt{5}$ m/s
(3) $100\sqrt{10}$ m/s
(4) $1000\sqrt{2}$ m/s

**Step-by-Step Solution:**
1.  **Recall Escape Velocity Formula:** Standard formula is $v_e = \sqrt{ rac{2GM}{R}}$.
2.  **Express in terms of Density ($
ho$):** Since the problem gives ratios of density and radius, substitute $M = 	ext{Volume} 	imes 	ext{Density} =  rac{4}{3}\pi R^3
ho$.
* $v_e = \sqrt{ rac{2G}{R} \left( rac{4}{3}\pi R^3
ho
ight)} = \sqrt{ rac{8}{3}\pi G
ho R^2} = R \sqrt{ rac{8}{3}\pi G
ho}$
* Therefore, $v_e \propto R \sqrt{
ho}$
3.  **Set up Ratio:**
* $ rac{v_{eB}}{v_{eA}} =  rac{R_B \sqrt{
ho_B}}{R_A \sqrt{
ho_A}} = \left( rac{R_B}{R_A}
ight) \sqrt{ rac{
ho_B}{
ho_A}}$
4.  **Substitute Given Values:** * $R_B = 0.1 R_A$ and $
ho_B = 0.1
ho_A$
* $ rac{v_{eB}}{10 \, km/s} = (0.1) \sqrt{0.1} =  rac{1}{10} 	imes  rac{1}{\sqrt{10}}$
5.  **Calculate Final Velocity:**
* $v_{eB} = 10 	imes  rac{1}{10\sqrt{10}} \, km/s =  rac{1}{\sqrt{10}} \, km/s$
* Convert to m/s: $v_{eB} =  rac{1000}{\sqrt{10}} = 100\sqrt{10} \, m/s$.

**Common Pitfalls & Misconceptions:**
* **Wrong proportionality:** Assuming $v_e \propto  rac{M}{R}$ directly without breaking $M$ down into density and volume. If a student assumes $v_e \propto \sqrt{
ho R}$ instead of $R\sqrt{
ho}$, they will get the wrong scaling factor.
* **Unit errors:** Forgetting to convert the final answer from km/s to m/s, matching the options.

**Final Answer:** (3) $100\sqrt{10}$ m/s

path: Detailed Step-by-Step Solutions: IIT JEE Mains Physics
Paper Reference: JEE-Main Session-1 (21-01-2026 / Morning Shift)
sections.text:
**Topic:** Thermodynamics & Calorimetry
**Question:** A gas based geyser heats water flowing at the rate of 5.0 litres per minute from $27^\circ C$ to $87^\circ C$. The rate of consumption of the gas is (in g/s):
(Take heat of combustion of gas = $5.0 	imes 10^4$ J/g, specific heat capacity of water = $4200$ J/kg·°C)
(1) 2.1
(2) 4.2
(3) 0.42
(4) 0.21

**Step-by-Step Solution:**
1.  **Calculate Water Mass Flow Rate:** * Volume flow rate = $5.0 \, L/min$.
* Since the density of water is $pprox 1 \, kg/L$, mass flow rate $ rac{dm}{dt} = 5.0 \, kg/min$.
* Convert to standard SI units (kg/s): $ rac{dm}{dt} =  rac{5.0}{60} \, kg/s =  rac{1}{12} \, kg/s$.
2.  **Calculate Required Heating Power:**
* Temperature change $\Delta T = 87^\circ C - 27^\circ C = 60^\circ C$.
* Required power $P_{req} =  rac{dm}{dt} \cdot c \cdot \Delta T = \left( rac{1}{12}
ight) 	imes 4200 	imes 60$
* $P_{req} = 4200 	imes 5 = 21000 \, J/s$ (or 21 kW).
3.  **Relate Power to Gas Consumption:**
* Let the rate of gas consumption be $x \, g/s$.
* Heat provided by gas per second $P_{gas} = x 	imes (	ext{Heat of Combustion}) = x 	imes 5.0 	imes 10^4 \, J/s$.
4.  **Equate and Solve:**
* $x 	imes 50000 = 21000$
* $x =  rac{21000}{50000} =  rac{21}{50} = 0.42 \, g/s$.

**Common Pitfalls & Misconceptions:**
* **Unit inconsistencies:** The most common mistake here is failing to convert minutes to seconds for the water flow rate, leading to an answer that is exactly 60 times too large (or too small depending on where the factor is applied).
* **Density of water:** Forgetting that 1 Litre of water = 1 kg.

**Final Answer:** (3) 0.42

sections.title: Question ID: JEE-2026-01-21-Q27

path: Detailed Step-by-Step Solutions: IIT JEE Mains Physics
Paper Reference: JEE-Main Session-1 (21-01-2026 / Morning Shift)
sections.text:
**Topic:** Alternating Current & EMI
**Question:** A conducting circular loop of area $1.0 \, m^2$ is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field which varies as $B = \sin(100 t)$ Tesla. If the resistance of the loop is $100 \, \Omega$, then the average thermal energy dissipated in the loop in one period is (in J):
(1) $ rac{\pi}{2}$
(2) $2\pi$
(3) $\pi$
(4) $\pi^2$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**
1.  **Calculate Magnetic Flux:** * $\Phi = B \cdot A = \sin(100t) 	imes 1.0 = \sin(100t) \, Wb$.
2.  **Calculate Induced EMF (Faraday's Law):**
* $arepsilon = - rac{d\Phi}{dt} = -  rac{d}{dt}[\sin(100t)] = -100 \cos(100t) \, V$.
3.  **Calculate Instantaneous Power:**
* $P(t) =  rac{arepsilon^2}{R} =  rac{[-100 \cos(100t)]^2}{100} =  rac{10000 \cos^2(100t)}{100} = 100 \cos^2(100t) \, W$.
4.  **Determine the Time Period:**
* The angular frequency is $\omega = 100 \, rad/s$.
* Time period $T =  rac{2\pi}{\omega} =  rac{2\pi}{100} =  rac{\pi}{50} \, s$.
5.  **Calculate Total Energy Dissipated in One Period:**
* $E = \int_0^T P(t) dt = \int_0^{\pi/50} 100 \cos^2(100t) dt$.
* We know the average value of $\cos^2(	heta)$ over a full cycle is $ rac{1}{2}$.
* Therefore, average power $\langle P
angle = 100 	imes  rac{1}{2} = 50 \, W$.
* Total energy $E = \langle P
angle 	imes T = 50 	imes  rac{\pi}{50} = \pi \, Joules$.

**Common Pitfalls & Misconceptions:**
* **Differentiation Chain Rule:** Forgetting to multiply by the inner derivative (100) when differentiating $\sin(100t)$, resulting in an EMF that is 100 times too small.
* **Integrating over 1 second instead of 1 period:** The prompt specifies "in one period". Some students automatically calculate average power and assume energy = power (which is true for 1 second, but not 1 period).
* **Trigonometric Average:** Not knowing that $\int_0^T \cos^2(\omega t) dt =  rac{T}{2}$, which forces students to manually expand $\cos^2$ into $ rac{1+\cos(2	heta)}{2}$ and waste valuable exam time.

**Final Answer:** (3) $\pi$

---
sections.title: Question ID: JEE-2026-01-21-Q28

path: Detailed Step-by-Step Solutions: IIT JEE Mains Physics
Paper Reference: JEE-Main Session-1 (21-01-2026 / Morning Shift)
sections.text:
**Topic:** Fluid Mechanics (Bernoulli's Principle & Continuity Equation)
**Question:** Water flows through a horizontal tube. The difference in height between the water columns in vertical tubes is 5 cm and the area of cross-sections at A and B are $6 \, cm^2$ and $3 \, cm^2$ respectively. The rate of flow will be (in $cm^3/s$, take $g=10 \, m/s^2$):
(1) $ rac{200}{\sqrt{3}}$
(2) $200\sqrt{6}$
(3) $200\sqrt{3}$
(4) $100\sqrt{3}$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**
1.  **Apply Continuity Equation:**
* $A_A v_A = A_B v_B$
* $6 \cdot v_A = 3 \cdot v_B \implies v_B = 2v_A$
2.  **Apply Bernoulli's Equation:**
* For a horizontal tube, potential energy head is constant.
* $P_A +  rac{1}{2}
ho v_A^2 = P_B +  rac{1}{2}
ho v_B^2$
* $P_A - P_B =  rac{1}{2}
ho (v_B^2 - v_A^2)$
3.  **Relate Pressure Difference to Height Difference:**
* The pressure difference manifests as the height difference $h$ in the vertical tubes: $P_A - P_B =
ho g h$.
* $
ho g h =  rac{1}{2}
ho ((2v_A)^2 - v_A^2) =  rac{1}{2}
ho (3v_A^2)$
* $gh =  rac{3}{2} v_A^2 \implies v_A = \sqrt{ rac{2gh}{3}}$
4.  **Mind the Units!**
* $g = 10 \, m/s^2 = 1000 \, cm/s^2$
* $h = 5 \, cm$
* $v_A = \sqrt{ rac{2 	imes 1000 	imes 5}{3}} = \sqrt{ rac{10000}{3}} =  rac{100}{\sqrt{3}} \, cm/s$
5.  **Calculate Flow Rate (Q):**
* $Q = A_A v_A = 6 	imes  rac{100}{\sqrt{3}} =  rac{600}{\sqrt{3}} = 200\sqrt{3} \, cm^3/s$

**Common Pitfalls & Misconceptions:**
* **Unit mismatch with gravity:** Often students plug in $g=10$ while keeping heights and areas in cm. This mixes meters and centimeters, yielding wild numbers like $\sqrt{100/3}$. $g$ must be converted to $cm/s^2$ (i.e., 1000) if working entirely in CGS.
* **Calculation slip:** Squaring $2v_A$ as $2v_A^2$ instead of $4v_A^2$, which leads to $v_B^2 - v_A^2 = v_A^2$ instead of $3v_A^2$.

**Final Answer:** (3) $200\sqrt{3}$

---
sections.title: Question ID: JEE-2026-01-21-Q29

path: Detailed Step-by-Step Solutions: IIT JEE Mains Physics
Paper Reference: JEE-Main Session-1 (21-01-2026 / Morning Shift)
sections.text:
**Topic:** Kinematics & Newton's Laws (Variable Force)
**Question:** A 4 kg mass moves under the influence of a force $ec{F} = (4t^3 \hat{i} - 3t \hat{j}) \, N$ where $t$ is the time in second. If mass starts from origin at $t=0$, the velocity and position after $t=2s$ will be:
(1) $ec{v} = 3\hat{i} +  rac{3}{2}\hat{j}$, $ec{r} =  rac{6}{5}\hat{i} + \hat{j}$
(2) $ec{v} = 4\hat{i} -  rac{3}{2}\hat{j}$, $ec{r} =  rac{8}{5}\hat{i} - \hat{j}$
(3) $ec{v} = 4\hat{i} +  rac{5}{2}\hat{j}$, $ec{r} =  rac{8}{5}\hat{i} + 2\hat{j}$
(4) $ec{v} = 4\hat{i} -  rac{3}{2}\hat{j}$, $ec{r} =  rac{6}{5}\hat{i} - \hat{j}$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**
1.  **Find Acceleration Vector:**
* $ec{a} =  rac{ec{F}}{m} =  rac{4t^3 \hat{i} - 3t \hat{j}}{4} = t^3 \hat{i} -  rac{3}{4}t \hat{j} \, m/s^2$.
2.  **Integrate to Find Velocity:**
* $ec{v}(t) = \int ec{a} \, dt = \int (t^3 \hat{i} -  rac{3}{4}t \hat{j}) \, dt =  rac{t^4}{4}\hat{i} -  rac{3}{8}t^2 \hat{j} + C$.
* Since it starts from origin from rest (implied by "starts from origin" combined with standard conventions if initial v isn't stated, though strictly "starts" implies $v(0)=0$), $C = 0$.
* At $t = 2s$: $ec{v}(2) =  rac{2^4}{4}\hat{i} -  rac{3}{8}(2^2)\hat{j} =  rac{16}{4}\hat{i} -  rac{12}{8}\hat{j} = 4\hat{i} - 1.5\hat{j} = 4\hat{i} -  rac{3}{2}\hat{j} \, m/s$.
3.  **Integrate to Find Position:**
* $ec{r}(t) = \int ec{v} \, dt = \int ( rac{t^4}{4}\hat{i} -  rac{3}{8}t^2 \hat{j}) \, dt =  rac{t^5}{20}\hat{i} -  rac{3}{24}t^3 \hat{j} + C'$.
* Starts at origin, so $ec{r}(0) = 0 \implies C' = 0$.
* At $t = 2s$: $ec{r}(2) =  rac{2^5}{20}\hat{i} -  rac{1}{8}(2^3)\hat{j} =  rac{32}{20}\hat{i} -  rac{8}{8}\hat{j} =  rac{8}{5}\hat{i} - \hat{j} \, m$.
4.  **Match with Options:**
* $ec{v} = 4\hat{i} -  rac{3}{2}\hat{j}$ and $ec{r} =  rac{8}{5}\hat{i} - \hat{j}$ matches option (2).

**Common Pitfalls & Misconceptions:**
* **Integration Errors:** Basic polynomial integration errors, especially forgetting to divide by the new exponent ($n+1$).
* **Forgetting to divide Force by Mass:** Many students integrate the Force vector directly, assuming it is the acceleration vector. Always verify $ec{a} = ec{F}/m$.

**Final Answer:** (2) $ec{v} = 4\hat{i} -  rac{3}{2}\hat{j}$, $ec{r} =  rac{8}{5}\hat{i} - \hat{j}$

---

path: Detailed Step-by-Step Solutions: IIT JEE Mains Physics
Paper Reference: JEE-Main Session-1 (21-01-2026 / Morning Shift)
sections.text:
**Topic:** Units and Dimensions
**Question:** Consider a modified Bernoulli equation:
$(P +  rac{A}{Bt^2}) +
ho g (h + Bt) +  rac{1}{2}
ho V^2 = 	ext{constant}$
If $t$ has the dimension of time, then the dimensions of A and B are:
(1) $[ML^0T^{-1}]$ and $[M^0LT]$
(2) $[ML^0T^{-1}]$ and $[M^0LT^{-1}]$
(3) $[ML^0T^{-2}]$ and $[M^0LT^{-2}]$
(4) $[ML^0T^{-2}]$ and $[M^0LT^{-1}]$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**
1.  **Principle of Dimensional Homogeneity:** Only physical quantities with the same dimensions can be added or subtracted.
2.  **Determine Dimension of B:**
* Look at the term $(h + Bt)$. $h$ is height, so it has dimensions of length $[L]$.
* Therefore, $Bt$ must also have dimensions of length $[L]$.
* $[B] \cdot [T] = [L] \implies [B] = [L T^{-1}]$. (This eliminates options 1 and 3).
3.  **Determine Dimension of A:**
* Look at the term $(P +  rac{A}{Bt^2})$. $P$ is pressure, which has dimensions $ rac{	ext{Force}}{	ext{Area}} =  rac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[L^2]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$.
* Therefore, $ rac{A}{Bt^2}$ must also have dimensions of pressure $[ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$.
* $ rac{[A]}{[L T^{-1}] \cdot [T^2]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$
* $ rac{[A]}{[LT]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$
* $[A] = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}] \cdot [LT] = [M L^{-1+1} T^{-2+1}] = [M L^0 T^{-1}]$.
4.  **Match with Options:**
* $[A] = [ML^0T^{-1}]$ and $[B] = [M^0LT^{-1}]$ matches option (2).

**Common Pitfalls & Misconceptions:**
* **Overcomplicating the algebra:** Expanding the whole Bernoulli expression instead of simply isolating the terms being added inside the brackets. The bracket $(h+Bt)$ is the fastest way to find $B$.
* **Recalling dimensions incorrectly:** Forgetting the dimensions of Pressure ($P = F/A$) is a frequent failure point.

**Final Answer:** (2) $[ML^0T^{-1}]$ and $[M^0LT^{-1}]$

sections.text: Chapter Two ELECTROSTATIC POTENTIAL AND CAPACITANCE 2.1 INTRODUCTION In Chapters 5 and 7 (Class XI), the notion of potential energy was introduced. When an external force does work in taking a body from a point to another against a force like spring force or gravitational force, that work gets stored as potential energy of the body. When the external force is removed, the body moves, gaining kinetic energy and losing an equal amount of potential energy. The sum of kinetic and potential energies is thus conserved. Forces of this kind are called conservative forces. Spring force and gravitational force are examples of conservative forces. Coulomb force between two (stationary) charges is also

sections.text: Chapter Three CURRENT ELECTRICITY 3.1 INTRODUCTION In Chapter 1, all charges whether free or bound, were considered to be at rest. Charges in motion constitute an electric current. Such currents occur naturally in many situations. Lightning is one such phenomenon in which charges flow from the clouds to the earth through the atmosphere, sometimes with disastrous results. The flow of charges in lightning is not steady, but in our everyday life we see many devices where charges flow in a steady manner, like water flowing smoothly in a river. sections.text: Chapter Four MOVING CHARGES AND MAGNETISM 4.1 INTRODUCTION Both Electricity and Magnetism have been known for more than 2000 years. However, it was only about 200 years ago, in 1820, that it was realised that they were intimately related. During a lecture demonstration in the summer of 1820, Danish physicist Hans Christian Oersted noticed that a current in a straight wire caused a noticeable deflection in a nearby magnetic compass needle. He investigated this phenomenon. He found that the alignment of the needle is tangential to an imaginary circle which has the straight wire as its centre and has its plane perpendicular to the wire. This situation is depicted in Fig.4.1(a). It is noticeable

sections.text: Physics Chapter Five MAGNETISM AND MATTER 5.1 INTRODUCTION Magnetic phenomena are universal in nature. Vast, distant galaxies, the tiny invisible atoms, humans and beasts all are permeated through and through with a host of magnetic fields from a variety of sources. The earth’s magnetism predates human evolution. The word magnet is derived from the name of an island in Greece called magnesia where magnetic ore deposits were found, as early as 600 BC. In the previous chapter we have learned that moving charges or electric currents produce magnetic fields. This discovery, which was made in the early part of the nineteenth century is credited to Oersted, Ampere, Biot and Savart, among others. I

sections.text: Physics Chapter Six ELECTROMAGNETIC INDUCTION 6.1 INTRODUCTION Electricity and magnetism were considered separate and unrelated phenomena for a long time. In the early decades of the nineteenth century, experiments on electric current by Oersted, Ampere and a few others established the fact that electricity and magnetism are inter-related. They found that moving electric charges produce magnetic fields. For example, an electric current deflects a magnetic compass needle placed in its vicinity. This naturally raises the questions like: Is the converse effect possible? Can moving magnets produce electric currents? Does the nature permit such a relation between electricity and magnetism? normalized_documents.normalized_text: chapter seven alternating current 7.1 introduction we have so far considered direct current (dc) sources and circuits with dc sources. these currents do not change direction with time. but voltages and currents that vary with time are very common. the electric mains supply in our homes and offices is a voltage that varies like a sine function with time. such a voltage is called alternating voltage (ac voltage) and the current driven by it in a circuit is called the alternating current (ac current)*. today, most of the electrical devices we use require ac voltage. this is mainly because most of the electrical energy sold by power companies is transmitted and distributed as alternating current

sections.text: Chapter Seven ALTERNATING CURRENT 7.1 INTRODUCTION We have so far considered direct current (dc) sources and circuits with dc sources. These currents do not change direction with time. But voltages and currents that vary with time are very common. The electric mains supply in our homes and offices is a voltage that varies like a sine function with time. Such a voltage is called alternating voltage (ac voltage) and the current driven by it in a circuit is called the alternating current (ac current)*. Today, most of the electrical devices we use require ac voltage. This is mainly because most of the electrical energy sold by power companies is transmitted and distributed as alternating current

Further, in Chapter 6, we have seen that a magnetic field changing with time gives rise to an electric field. Is the converse also true? Does an electric field changing with time give rise to a magnetic field? James Clerk Maxwell (1831-1879), argued that this was indeed the case – not only an electric current but also a time-varying electric field generates magnetic field. sections.text: Chapter Nine RAY OPTICS AND OPTICAL INSTRUMENTS 9.1 INTRODUCTION Nature has endowed the human eye (retina) with the sensitivity to detect electromagnetic waves within a small range of the electromagnetic spectrum. Electromagnetic radiation belonging to this region of the spectrum (wavelength of about 400 nm to 750 nm) is called light. It is mainly through light and the sense of vision that we know and interpret the world around us. There are two things that we can intuitively mention about light from common experience. sections.text: Wave Optics Chapter Ten WAVE OPTICS 10.1 INTRODUCTION In 1637 Descartes gave the corpuscular model of light and derived Snell’s law. It explained the laws of reflection and refraction of light at an interface. The corpuscular model predicted that if the ray of light (on refraction) bends towards the normal then the speed of light would be greater in the second medium. This corpuscular model of light was further developed by Isaac Newton in his famous book entitled OPTICKS and because of the tremendous popularity of this book, the corpuscular model is very often attributed to Newton.
